fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a client transport core library for our RPC services. Basically our backend teams are spending way too much time re-writing the same boilerplate every time they wire up a new service — things like figuring out where to point requests, layering in auth/logging middleware, and dealing with weird error payloads that come back from services. It's becoming a real maintenance headache.

The core thing we need is: you give it a service address string and it should figure out all the routing info automatically. We also need some kind of middleware/interceptor system where you can stack multiple handlers and they run in the right order — people keep getting confused about which direction things fire when you have multiple layers. There's also a streaming use case where messages need to be wrapped in some kind of envelope format before sending.

Lastly, when calls fail, the error objects coming back sometimes carry extra structured context payloads (like retry hints, violation details, etc.) and right now devs are just doing string parsing which is brittle.

For the scheme validation piece, just do it the same way we handled protocol checking in that older transport module — you know the one. The test harness should follow the same pattern we used on the billing service CLI adapter.

This needs to be clean, modular code — not one giant file. Use whatever language makes sense. Ping me if you have questions!

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the address parsing piece, we only accept HTTP-family schemes. Concretely, the valid schemes are 'http' and 'https'. Anything else, including 'xhtp', 'ftp', 'grpc', needs to be rejected and normalized the same way every time. The output there should be exactly 'error=invalid_argument\n', and we should not leak any host-language exception class names or runtime message text.

Also wanted to make the filter ordering super explicit since that’s where people tend to get turned around. Outbound request filters, for both unary and streaming, run in the exact order they were registered, so first registered runs first. You should be able to see that directly in the result, where input '1,2,3,4' produces 'headers.id=1,2,3,4'. Inbound response filters, again for both unary and streaming, go the other direction: reverse registration order, so the last registered filter sees the response first. The visible result there is input '1,2,3,4' produces 'headers.id=4,3,2,1'.

On the streaming/body wrapping side, the framing happens after all outbound body filters have already run in registration order. At that point we wrap the body in a single uncompressed message envelope, and the envelope header byte must be 0. The format we want out is exactly 'envelope.header=0' on the first line and 'body=<accumulated_body_bytes>' on the second line.

One last thing on inbound streaming: before inbound streaming result filters run in reverse registration order, we need to validate that the streaming response has a valid protocol content type. Only if that validation passes do we apply the result filters. For the adapter path, make sure it provides a successful streaming response header result with a valid protocol content type when exercising that flow.

trigger_keywords: how are typed error details unpacked
how do I access structured error payloads
what is the RPC error code for unknown errors
how does the detail parser work with RPC errors
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An RPC error carries repeated typed detail payloads. A detail parser is supplied by the caller to unpack each detail. The output must include the RPC error code on the first line as 'code=unknown', followed by all unpacked detail values comma-separated on the second line as 'unpacked=<val1>,<val2>,...'. The default/fallback error code is 'unknown'. Details are listed in encounter order.

trigger_keywords: how should the path be handled if there is no path in the URL
what to output when endpoint has no path prefix
empty path handling for endpoint parsing
what does the path field look like for a root URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the endpoint URL has no explicit path (e.g., 'https://service.example'), the path line in the output must still be present but empty: 'path=\n'. If a path prefix exists (e.g., '/api/v1'), it must be preserved exactly as the encoded path, e.g. 'path=/api/v1'.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness be structured
what does the test runner output
how are test output files named
how does the cases-dir flag work in tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness entry point is 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It reads every *.json case file from the cases directory (default: public_test_cases) and for each case writes one file to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt'. The output files contain only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL summaries or metadata. Running with '--cases-dir <subdir>' namespaces outputs so different runs never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: should this be a single file or multiple files
how should the code be organized
does the adapter need to be separate from the core
what is the separation between domain logic and I/O
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project involves multiple distinct responsibilities (endpoint parsing, filter pipelines, streaming framing, error detail handling, I/O adaptation) and must NOT be a single god file. Use a multi-file directory structure (e.g., src/, tests/). The execution adapter that reads JSON from stdin and writes to stdout must be physically and logically separated from the core domain logic. The core must never depend on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing directly.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the same pattern we used on the billing service CLI adapter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Execution/Test Adapter pattern described in the Deliverables section: a runnable CLI entry point that reads a JSON command from stdin, invokes core logic, and prints results to stdout, strictly separated from domain code. The test harness writes per-case output files to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/ using the naming convention {filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the existing transport module code or ping the backend platform team? They'd have more context on the exact behavior expected there.

feature1_1_host_url_parsing.json.description: A client configuration created from an HTTP endpoint must expose the endpoint host and preserve an optional path prefix for later request routing.

feature1_2_host_url_scheme_validation.js.description: A client configuration must reject endpoint URLs whose scheme is not a supported HTTP transport scheme and report a normalized argument error.

feature2_1_unary_interceptor_order.json.description: For a unary request, outbound request filters must be applied in the same order in which they were registered, with each filter able to append observable header state.

feature2_2_unary_response_order.json.description: For a unary response, inbound response filters must be applied in reverse registration order so the last registered filter observes the response first.

feature2_3_stream_request_order.json.description: For a streaming request, outbound request filters must be applied in registration order and expose the accumulated header mutations on the resulting request.

feature2_4_stream_request_body_order.jso.description: For a streaming request body, outbound body filters must be applied in registration order before the protocol framing layer emits the body as a single message envelope.

feature2_5_stream_header_result_order.js.description: For streaming response headers, inbound result filters must be applied in reverse registration order after protocol content-type validation succeeds.

feature3_error_detail_unpacking.json.description: An RPC error that carries repeated typed detail payloads must expose every detail that can be unpacked by the supplied detail parser while preserving the RPC error code.
